title: Shift-Change Exploitation with Coordinated Evening Burst Activity
narrative: Fraudsters exploit 11 PM to 1 AM window during fraud team shift handover, executing rapid-fire CNP transactions that span two operational periods to delay detection consolidation. A compromised card shows dormancy for 8-14 days, then suddenly fires 12-18 transactions between 23:15-00:45 targeting digital goods (ProductCD='W') with amounts clustering $47-$89. Transactions deliberately straddle midnight (6-8 before, 6-10 after) so automated daily reconciliation splits the pattern across two reporting cycles. The attacker uses 3-4 distinct P_emaildomains rotating every 20-35 minutes, all temporary providers, with matching addr1 values but incrementing card1_txn_count_1hr from 0 to 17. CVV matches decline after transaction 5, suggesting card-testing completion. R_emaildomain remains consistent across burst while shipping addresses (dist1) show 890-2400 mile variance, indicating digital fulfillment or mule network activation during minimal analyst oversight.
indicators: 12-18 transactions concentrated in 23:15-00:45 window with 6+ before midnight, 6+ after
Card dormant 8-14 days then card1_txn_count_1hr escalates 0â†’17 within 90 minutes

3-4 distinct P_emaildomains rotate every 20-35 minutes, all temporary email providers
CVV match rate drops from 100% to 0% after transaction 5 in sequence
dist1 variance 890-2400 miles across transactions while addr1 remains constant
is_night=1 for entire burst with txn_hour transitioning from 23â†’00â†’01
summary: A card sits idle for over a week, then suddenly fires 12-18 transactions in a 90-minute window spanning midnight, with amounts between $47-$89 all targeting digital products. Email addresses rotate every 20-35 minutes while shipping distances vary wildly despite a constant billing address, and CVV matches stop after the fifth transaction.
reasoning: The attacker times the burst across the midnight boundary to split the pattern across two daily reports, exploiting reduced analyst coverage during shift changes. The dormancy-then-frenzy pattern combined with email rotation and failed CVV checks reveals card testing followed by immediate monetization before the compromise is detected.
indicator_explanations: Attack targets midnight shift change when monitoring gaps are widest
Card suddenly erupts from complete inactivity to extreme burst pattern
All amounts fall in narrow range suggesting automated script control
Throwaway email addresses swap out every half hour during the attack
Fraudster stops providing correct CVV after initial authorization attempts succeed
Transactions bounce across distant locations while billing address stays frozen
Entire sequence executes during overnight hours spanning midnight crossover point
